what_measured: motivation to blame: task choice frequency, effort in clicking task, persistence across escalating demands
experiment: Participants read vignettes describing harm (nursing home medication errors) framed as intentional or unintentional, then freely chose between blaming/condemning the harm-doer or alternative tasks (Exp. 1) or completed a reinforcement game where clicking earned signatures on a petition to investigate the harm-doer, with escalating response requirements (Exp. 2). Motivation indexed by choice propensity and clicking effort/persistence.

what_manipulated: third-party intervention possibility and risk; incentive structure for third parties
what_measured: cooperation rate among disputants; third-party intervention rate; earnings
experiment: Multi-party game paradigm (Peacemaker Game) where two disputants choose cooperation or competition and third parties choose whether to intervene by introducing side payments. In Experiments 1-3, varied whether third-party intervention is possible/probable. In Experiment 4, varied consequence structure for third parties (loss-only, gain-only, gain-or-loss, fixed-payoff). Experiments 5-6 used recall tasks rating perceived costs/benefits of past intervention decisions.

what_manipulated: postural expansiveness (expanded vs. contracted body position in photographs/interactions)
what_measured: romantic attraction; odds of receiving 'yes' response in speed dating; mediation path through perceived dominance/openness
experiment: Study 1: coded naturally occurring posture in 4-minute speed-dating interactions; measured whether partner wanted to see person again. Study 2a: manipulated posture in online dating profiles (expanded vs. contracted photographs) across 3,000 swipes, counted 'yes' matches. Study 2b: rated manipulated photographs for perceived dominance and openness, tested mediation. No stimulus timing, sample size, or effect magnitude reported in skeleton.

what_manipulated: economic inequality level (state-level Gini coefficient in survey; high vs. low inequality in experiment)
experiment: Survey: participants from 1,498 respondents across US states completed dictator game task (allocating raffle tickets) while residing in areas with measured income inequality. Experiment: 704 MTurk participants viewed pie chart portraying state wealth distribution as high or low inequality, then participated in dictator game. Key test: income × inequality interaction predicting generosity across both designs.

what_manipulated: target race (black vs. white patient/person) and participant false belief endorsement about biological differences
what_measured: pain perception ratings; treatment recommendation accuracy; false belief endorsement
experiment: Study 1: 92 white laypersons rated pain across 18 scenarios for self, then randomly assigned to rate pain for matched-gender black or white target; completed false belief measure. Study 2: 222 white medical students/residents read mock medical cases (black and white patient) with identical presentations, rated pain and made treatment recommendations, completed false belief measure. Interaction tested: target race × false belief endorsement predicting pain and treatment accuracy.

what_manipulated: volitional information hiding (choose-not-to-answer vs. full disclosure vs. inadvertent nondisclosure)
what_measured: preference/hiring choice; trust levels (money sent in trust game); trustworthiness perception; predicted grade
experiment: Experiments 1-2: participants viewed dating profiles with incomplete responses (hider: selected 'choose not to answer'; revealer: answered all questions; inadvertent nondiscloser: system prevented display) and rated dating interest. Experiment 3a: trust game where senders allocated money to receivers who had revealed or hidden personal information. Experiment 3b: evaluated job candidates' revealed vs. hidden grades, rated trustworthiness, selected hire. Experiments 4a-4b: prospective employees decided to reveal or hide drug use; employers rated candidates; focal test of actor-observer discrepancy.
